chunk_summary: Case-based management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) focuses on discharge criteria, outpatient care, and addressing complications like infections, feeding issues, and caregiver reliability for effective recovery and follow-up.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of using admission criteria and edema-free stability for discharge decisions in SAM management.
Learn to assess and address feeding effectiveness in infants with low weight-for-length and weak suck.
Develop strategies for tracing and managing defaulters in outpatient CMAM programs.
Recognize and manage complicated SAM cases with infections, hypoxia, or suspected HIV.
key_facts: Discharge should require repeated edema-free stability; discordance between WHZ and MUAC is common.
MUAC <125 mm indicates continued therapy despite WHZ improvement.
Infants with weak suck require urgent lactation support, expressed breast milk plans, and feeding effectiveness assessment.
Defaulters in outpatient CMAM must be traced immediately; weight loss, edema, or danger signs warrant urgent referral.
Severe pneumonia with hypoxia in SAM requires broad empiric antibiotics, high-dose cotrimoxazole for PCP, and HIV testing with ART initiation after stabilization.
reference_quote: Correct approach is to anchor recovery to the admission criterion and require repeated edema-free stability; discordance is common and continued therapy with close follow-up is safer than single-metric discharge. (Source: NITI Aayog, Community-based management of acute malnutrition in India report, 2023)
document_key_topics: Outpatient care and follow-up in CMAM
Feeding effectiveness in infants with SAM
Management of complicated SAM cases with infections or HIV
